module_title: AI Ethics and Responsible Use of AI-Q
companion_learning: AI Ethics and Responsible Use of AI-L

questions.stem: Which best defines algorithmic bias in AI systems?

options.A: The speed penalty incurred by running AI on older hardware
options.B: Systematic, unfair discrimination in AI outputs caused by biased training data, model design, or evaluation metrics
options.C: The tendency of AI systems to prefer alphabetically first answers
options.D: Random variation in model predictions due to stochastic training

questions.explanation: Algorithmic bias is systematic and unfair — it consistently disadvantages certain groups. Sources include biased historical data, biased feature selection, or biased evaluation metrics. It is not random noise (Jobin et al., Nature MI, 2019).
questions.source: Jobin et al. The global landscape of AI ethics guidelines. Nature Machine Intelligence, 2019. https://arxiv.org/abs/1906.11668

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT one of the EU High-Level Expert Group's seven requirements for Trustworthy AI?

options.A: Technical robustness and safety
options.B: Privacy and data governance
options.C: Maximum commercial profitability
options.D: Accountability

questions.explanation: The EU HLEG's seven requirements are: (1) Human agency & oversight, (2) Technical robustness & safety, (3) Privacy & data governance, (4) Transparency, (5) Diversity, non-discrimination & fairness, (6) Societal & environmental wellbeing, (7) Accountability. Commercial profitability is not among them.
questions.source: EU HLEG. Ethics Guidelines for Trustworthy AI. 2019. https://digital-strategy.ec.europa.eu/en/library/ethics-guidelines-trustworthy-ai

questions.stem: Why is transparency distinct from explainability in AI ethics?

options.A: Transparency and explainability are synonyms used interchangeably
options.B: Transparency refers to openness about how a system works at a systemic level; explainability is the ability to provide a human-understandable reason for a specific decision
options.C: Transparency applies only to open-source models; explainability only to commercial models
options.D: Transparency is a legal term; explainability is a technical term with no legal relevance

questions.explanation: Transparency: stakeholders can understand the overall system — its data, design, and deployment context. Explainability: a specific output can be interpreted by a human. GDPR Article 22 mandates explainability for automated decisions affecting individuals.
questions.source: EU HLEG. Ethics Guidelines for Trustworthy AI. 2019; GDPR Article 22. https://gdpr-info.eu/art-22-gdpr

questions.stem: How does individual fairness differ from group fairness (demographic parity) as a definition?

options.A: Individual fairness applies to humans; group fairness applies only to corporations
options.B: Individual fairness requires similar individuals to be treated similarly regardless of group membership; demographic parity requires equal positive outcome rates across groups
options.C: Individual fairness is always achievable alongside group fairness
options.D: Individual fairness measures accuracy; group fairness measures precision

questions.explanation: Individual fairness (Dwork et al., 2012): similar individuals → similar outcomes. Demographic parity: positive prediction rates equal across demographic groups. Chouldechova (2017) proved these are mathematically incompatible when base rates differ — the Impossibility Theorem.
questions.source: Jobin et al. Nature MI, 2019; Chouldechova. Fair prediction with disparate impact. 2017.

questions.stem: The Gender Shades study (Buolamwini & Gebru, 2018) found error rates of ~0.7% for lighter-skinned men and ~34.7% for darker-skinned women. Identify the bias type, root cause, and one technically valid mitigation.

options.A: Deployment bias; caused by users misusing the tool; fix: write better user manuals
options.B: Representation bias in training data; datasets overrepresented lighter-skinned male faces; fix: curate balanced training datasets across skin tone and gender, re-evaluate with disaggregated metrics
options.C: Measurement bias; thermometers measuring skin tone were faulty; fix: replace hardware
options.D: Feedback loop bias; darker-skinned users submit more images; fix: rate-limit API calls by demographic group

questions.explanation: Buolamwini & Gebru identified representation bias: training datasets (IJB-A, Adience) skewed heavily toward lighter-skinned males. Fix: (1) balanced dataset curation; (2) disaggregated accuracy reporting by subgroup; (3) ongoing bias audits. Rate-limiting by demographics would itself be discriminatory.
questions.source: Buolamwini & Gebru. Gender Shades. PMLR FAccT, 2018. http://proceedings.mlr.press/v81/buolamwini18a.html

questions.stem: What is 'historical bias' in AI training data?

options.A: Data that is more than 10 years old and therefore outdated
options.B: Bias that arises when training data reflects past societal inequalities or discriminatory practices, encoding them into model predictions
options.C: Errors introduced when digitising historical paper records
options.D: Bias caused by selecting training data in alphabetical order

questions.explanation: Historical bias occurs when training data reflects past social inequalities — e.g., hiring data from when women were systematically excluded. Models trained on this data learn and perpetuate those patterns even when protected attributes are removed (Mehrabi et al., 2021).
questions.source: Mehrabi et al. A Survey on Bias and Fairness in ML. ACM CSUR, 2021. https://arxiv.org/abs/1908.09635

questions.stem: In Obermeyer et al. (2019), what proxy variable caused racial bias and by how many percentage points were Black patients under-scored?

options.A: Age as proxy for illness severity; 15 pp
options.B: Healthcare cost as proxy for medical need; Black patients scored 26.3 pp lower than equally ill white patients
options.C: ZIP code as proxy for income; 40 pp
options.D: Insurance type as proxy for health status; 10 pp

questions.explanation: The algorithm used healthcare cost as a proxy for medical need. Because Black patients historically incurred lower costs due to systemic barriers to care, they were assigned lower risk scores than equally ill white patients — a 26.3 pp gap. Replacing cost with illness severity reduced the disparity by 84%.
questions.source: Obermeyer et al. Dissecting racial bias in an algorithm. Science, 2019. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31649194

questions.stem: Why is 'fairness through unawareness' (removing protected attributes) insufficient to eliminate bias?

options.A: It is sufficient — removing protected attributes always eliminates all bias
options.B: Other features (ZIP code, education, income) are correlated with protected attributes and serve as proxies, allowing models to infer and discriminate indirectly
options.C: It increases bias because models then rely on random noise
options.D: It violates GDPR by deleting legally required demographic data

questions.explanation: Proxy discrimination: features correlated with protected attributes (e.g., ZIP code correlated with race due to redlining; names correlated with gender/ethnicity) allow models to discriminate indirectly. True fairness requires causal and counterfactual analysis, not just attribute removal.
questions.source: Barocas & Hardt. Fairness in Machine Learning. NIPS Tutorial, 2017. https://fairmlbook.org

questions.stem: The COMPAS algorithm had equal overall accuracy for Black and white defendants, yet ProPublica found racial bias. How can both claims be simultaneously true?

options.A: They cannot both be true — mathematical impossibility
options.B: Both use different fairness metrics: COMPAS optimised for equal overall accuracy; ProPublica measured false positive rates (45% for Black vs 23% for white defendants), revealing disparate error distribution
options.C: ProPublica's analysis was methodologically flawed
options.D: Equal accuracy means no bias by any definition — the claims contradict each other

questions.explanation: Chouldechova's Impossibility Theorem (2017): when base rates differ across groups, it is mathematically impossible to simultaneously satisfy equal accuracy, equal FPR, and equal FNR. COMPAS achieved equal overall accuracy at the cost of higher FPR for Black defendants. Both analyses used different, legitimate fairness criteria.
questions.source: Angwin et al. Machine Bias. ProPublica, 2016; Chouldechova. Fair prediction with disparate impact. 2017.

questions.stem: A bank's AI loan model shows 91% overall accuracy but approves 78% of white applicants and only 52% of Black applicants with identical financial profiles. Diagnose the fairness failure and propose two technically valid remediations.

options.A: The model is fair — 91% accuracy exceeds most regulatory thresholds
options.B: The model exhibits disparate impact (demographic parity violation). Remediations: (1) Apply reweighing or re-sampling to balance approval rates during training; (2) Use adversarial debiasing to penalise predictions correlated with race
options.C: No technical fix exists — replace with human decision-making
options.D: The disparity is acceptable because financial profiles may differ in uncaptured ways

questions.explanation: Identical financial profiles + 26 pp approval gap = disparate impact. Technical fixes: (1) Reweighing: higher sample weights for under-represented approval cases; (2) Adversarial debiasing (Zhang et al., 2018): train an adversary to predict race from model outputs and penalise the main model. Both require ongoing disaggregated auditing.
questions.source: Mehrabi et al. ACM CSUR, 2021; Zhang et al. Mitigating unwanted biases with adversarial learning. AIES, 2018.

mini_quizzes.title: MQ-3 · EU Trustworthy AI and AI Act

questions.stem: Under the EU AI Act (2024), which AI application is classified as 'unacceptable risk' and therefore banned?

options.A: AI-powered spam filters
options.B: Real-time public facial recognition by law enforcement (with narrow exceptions)
options.C: AI recommendation systems on streaming platforms
options.D: AI-powered grammar checkers

questions.explanation: The EU AI Act classifies real-time biometric identification in public spaces by law enforcement as unacceptable risk — prohibited except for narrow exceptions (e.g., searching for missing children, preventing imminent terrorist attacks).
questions.source: EU AI Act, Regulation (EU) 2024/1689, Articles 5 & 6. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=CELEX:32024R1689

questions.stem: Which of the four NIST AI RMF functions establishes organisational policies and accountability structures for AI risk management?

options.A: MAP
options.B: MEASURE
options.C: MANAGE
options.D: GOVERN

questions.explanation: GOVERN establishes organisational policies, roles, responsibilities, and culture for AI risk management. MAP identifies context and potential harms; MEASURE analyses risks; MANAGE prioritises and treats them. GOVERN is the foundation enabling the other three functions.
questions.source: NIST. AI Risk Management Framework 1.0. 2023. https://airc.nist.gov/RMF

questions.stem: How does the EU AI Act's risk-based approach categorise AI systems, and what does 'high-risk' classification impose?

options.A: All AI systems are treated equally regardless of harm potential
options.B: Systems are classified as unacceptable, high, limited, or minimal risk; high-risk systems require conformity assessment, risk management, data governance, transparency, human oversight, and accuracy documentation before deployment
options.C: Only AI systems handling financial data are regulated
options.D: High-risk classification requires AI systems to be open-source

questions.explanation: Four-tier risk framework: Unacceptable (banned), High-risk (strict obligations), Limited (transparency obligations), Minimal (no specific obligations). High-risk systems — Annex III: CV screening, credit scoring, medical devices, law enforcement — must pass conformity assessment, implement risk management, document training data, and ensure human oversight.
questions.source: EU AI Act, Regulation (EU) 2024/1689, Articles 6–50 & Annex III. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=CELEX:32024R1689

questions.stem: How does the EU HLEG requirement for 'human agency and oversight' differ from 'accountability'?

options.A: They are identical requirements phrased differently
options.B: Human agency & oversight ensures humans can understand, intervene in, and override AI decisions; accountability assigns responsibility for outcomes to identifiable human actors or organisations
options.C: Human agency applies to users; accountability only to developers
options.D: Accountability is a technical metric; human agency is philosophical with no regulatory force

questions.explanation: Human agency & oversight: AI must support human decision-making, allow intervention, and include override mechanisms — critical for high-stakes domains. Accountability: responsibility must be attributable via audit trails and documentation to specific persons or organisations. Both are required; neither subsumes the other.
questions.source: EU HLEG. Ethics Guidelines for Trustworthy AI. 2019. https://digital-strategy.ec.europa.eu/en/library/ethics-guidelines-trustworthy-ai

questions.stem: A hospital proposes deploying an AI triage system in the emergency department. Using the EU HLEG's seven requirements, identify the three most critical and justify your selection.

options.A: Commercial profitability, processing speed, and storage efficiency
options.B: Technical robustness & safety (system failures risk patient harm), accountability (responsibility must be attributed if errors cause harm), and human agency & oversight (clinicians must be able to override AI recommendations)
options.C: Diversity & non-discrimination only — all other requirements are secondary
options.D: Only privacy & data governance matters — HIPAA compliance is sufficient

questions.explanation: In a life-critical deployment: (1) Technical robustness & safety: failures or false negatives could cause preventable deaths. (2) Accountability: clear responsibility chains for adverse outcomes. (3) Human agency & oversight: override capability is non-negotiable in emergency medicine. All seven requirements apply, but these three are highest-stakes.
questions.source: EU HLEG. Ethics Guidelines for Trustworthy AI. 2019; EU AI Act Annex III. 2024.

questions.stem: What is SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) in the context of explainable AI?

options.A: A regulatory standard for AI model documentation published by NIST
options.B: A post-hoc explainability method that assigns each feature a contribution value to a specific prediction, based on Shapley values from cooperative game theory
options.C: A neural network architecture designed to be inherently transparent
options.D: A data augmentation technique that improves model fairness

questions.explanation: SHAP (Lundberg & Lee, 2017) uses Shapley values from cooperative game theory to compute each feature's marginal contribution to a specific model prediction. It satisfies efficiency, symmetry, dummy, and additivity axioms. Relevant to GDPR Article 22 for automated decisions.
questions.source: Lundberg & Lee. A Unified Approach to Interpreting Model Predictions. NeurIPS, 2017. https://arxiv.org/abs/1705.07874

questions.stem: What is the primary trade-off between model accuracy and explainability in AI system design?

options.A: More accurate models are always more explainable
options.B: Simple models (linear regression, decision trees) are more explainable but typically less accurate than complex models (deep neural networks) which are harder to interpret
options.C: Explainability has no relationship to model accuracy
options.D: Model accuracy decreases as training data increases

questions.explanation: The accuracy-explainability trade-off: linear regression and shallow decision trees offer high interpretability at the cost of representational capacity. Deep neural networks achieve state-of-the-art accuracy but are black boxes. Post-hoc tools (SHAP, LIME) partially bridge this gap.
questions.source: EU HLEG. Ethics Guidelines for Trustworthy AI. 2019; NIST AI RMF. 2023.

questions.stem: Why does GDPR Article 22 create a legal obligation relevant to AI explainability in the EU?

options.A: It bans all automated decision-making in the EU
options.B: It gives individuals the right not to be subject to solely automated decisions with significant effects, and the right to obtain a meaningful explanation of such decisions
options.C: It requires all AI models to be open-source
options.D: It mandates a minimum accuracy threshold of 95% for AI systems affecting EU citizens

questions.explanation: GDPR Article 22 grants EU data subjects: (1) the right to object to solely automated decisions with significant effects (credit, hiring, insurance); (2) the right to obtain 'meaningful information about the logic involved.' This creates a de facto explainability mandate for high-stakes AI systems in the EU.
questions.source: EU GDPR Article 22. https://gdpr-info.eu/art-22-gdpr; Selbst & Barocas. The Intuitive Appeal of Explainable Machines. Fordham Law Review, 2018.

questions.stem: How does the NIST AI RMF's MEASURE function operationalise bias assessment in a deployed AI system?

options.A: MEASURE requires organisations to delete all training data after model deployment
options.B: MEASURE involves quantitatively analysing AI risks including bias (disparate impact, equal opportunity metrics), robustness, and explainability, using metrics aligned to the system's context and stakeholders
options.C: MEASURE only applies to systems with more than 1 million users
options.D: MEASURE is a one-time audit conducted before deployment only

questions.explanation: NIST AI RMF MEASURE function: organisations select and apply metrics appropriate to context — bias metrics (demographic parity difference, equal opportunity difference), robustness metrics (performance under distribution shift), explainability metrics. It is ongoing — not just pre-deployment — and feeds into MANAGE for risk prioritisation.
questions.source: NIST. AI Risk Management Framework 1.0. 2023. https://airc.nist.gov/RMF

questions.stem: A CV-screening AI rejects 70% of applications from women vs 30% of men in technical roles. Using NIST AI RMF, outline one concrete action per function (GOVERN, MAP, MEASURE, MANAGE).

options.A: No action needed — the disparity reflects real differences in qualified applicants
options.B: GOVERN: appoint an AI ethics officer with authority to halt deployment; MAP: identify affected stakeholders and document high-risk context; MEASURE: compute gender-disaggregated approval rates and equal opportunity difference; MANAGE: retrain with balanced data and set gender-parity constraints
options.C: Only MANAGE is needed — immediately retrain the model
options.D: Only MEASURE is needed — publish bias metrics and take no further action

questions.explanation: All four RMF functions required: GOVERN establishes accountability. MAP contextualises harm — EU AI Act Annex III: CV screening = high-risk. MEASURE quantifies: equal opportunity difference = TPR_male − TPR_female; disparate impact = approval_female / approval_male. MANAGE: retraining, fairness constraints, ongoing monitoring, regulatory notification where required.
questions.source: NIST. AI Risk Management Framework 1.0. 2023; EU AI Act Annex III. 2024.
